item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded: Good idea, added

item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded: Can we change the hyperlinked text to 'amnestic treatment' instead? After all, the Tale refers to all lettered types of amnestics.

Frankly speaking, I am quite surprised that this made it this far. It came in a rather low position among contest entries, which dampened my spirits for a while. And yet, it climbed up over other works that I felt more pride towards.

item.title: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Edit: timer expired, edits made

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ show block
– hide block

This skip mentions the usage of "Class-E Amnestics" to eliminate the effects of SCP-3434. According to the [[updated-amnestics-guide|Updated Amnestics Guide]], Class E amnestics are used to make people think that anomalous things are normal. However, a new reader might not pick up on that. So I propose linking the aforementioned guide.

Editing this:

All aforementioned behaviours will cease upon Class E amnestic treatment.

To this:

All aforementioned behaviours will cease upon Class E amnestic treatment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Honestly, I'm not a big fan of this one, for reasons other people have already stated. The idea of babies attempting to stay in the womb is a really strong concept, but the twist that they're former Foundation employees reincarnated sort of robs it of its strength. It feels cheap, especially with all the crosslinks. The crosslinks feel like cheap attempts to link this story to more popular things.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Did you remember?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 because the effects themselves are horrifying and make me even more terrified of pregnancy than I already was.

item.title: Re: I like it but..
item.content:encoded:
Alpha-1's job is to be at the beak and call of the O5 Council. Provided that the anomaly can essentially expose Foundation operations to the public and suggest the fate of some of their personnel (to be reborn as an E-3434 instance), the O5 wants to ensure secrecy even from most of the Foundation.

Thus, Alpha-1 is called upon.

item.title: I like it but..
item.content:encoded: Staff notice: this user has been permanently banned for sockpuppeting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, I can see benefit in it, a lot of interesting 'what if?' questions can be asked when sending someone to essentially the same death twice, such as:

Will the Church know?

Will it interrupt their ritual?

Will other previous memories surface?

Will those other memories allow him to avoid or disrupt the ritual if he gets captured, or allow him to remember parts of the place/people?

And so on; live, die, repeat. People would want to see if it's useful.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Even if it's just headcanon, someone dying that way twice implies, to me at least, that someone knew about it, got them to do the same thing with the Church (knowing they'd done it before), and they died the same way.

Frankly sounds like an over-the-top arrangement.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Mmm, to me I see there as being some sort of sinister reason behind it; it couldn't be a coincidence. Even if it's just headcanon, someone dying that way twice implies, to me at least, that someone knew about it, got them to do the same thing with the Church (knowing they'd done it before), and they died the same way. To me that's quite chilling, it's even more chilling if the Foundation's position of them being former personnel is true, as it means they hired them again, and sent them to the same death, while knowing about the fact they'd done it before. But I think we just see it differently.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I hope you won't mind if I keep it as headcanon

I don't mind, but it's painfully crappy if one dies the same way twice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh really? I thought he died the same way. Personally, I actually prefer that, it seems a bit more chilling, I hope you won't mind if I keep it as headcanon. I would imagine that the memories of previous deaths wouldn't carry on to more than one, as they would just become memories of memories from a previous life, which I find unlikely. But, then again, just my headcanon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
the poor person who died twice to the same thing

Technically, he didn't die twice to the same thing. The memory of that one first death dragged on in subsequent lives. For that person, I summarise that he died of mundane ways in his second life.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Now this, I do like. For me the idea of reincarnation has always been an appealing one, even if I don't actually believe in it, so this really clicked with me. I've always thought, would you keep attributes of yourself into another life; your average emotional state, maybe even some very deep rooted memories? And that's also why I liked this, these people remember those deaths, and do not wish to return to the world, especially the poor person who died twice to the same thing, I wouldn't blame them for not coming into the light again. And even that they need a class-E amnestic to make them forget, that's some serious treatment. I also like the title's nod to the, one of the, Foundation's mottos: 'We die in the dark so you can live in the light.' That's nice because it really does show the appeal of the dark, of the danger of knowledge, the safety of ignorance.

And yet, while what's here is enough for an upvote from me, but I feel as if even more could be added to this. Perhaps not in the article itself, but outside it, with the idea it has.

The only criticism I have is that some of the interview logs seemed a bit ham-fisted.

E-3434-48: I think I will remain here, in the dark.

You'd think they'd at least slur the 'I will' too an 'I'll'. But other than that, I really like this.

item.title: Re: Thanks, Attribution, and Others
item.content:encoded: I thought of it as something like that the minute I saw the shortcut to SCP-343; out of all the shortcuts, that was the only one I saw prior to looking at this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Considering that it affects humans across lives (from one reincarnation to another), the cause would be more spiritual in nature than anything else. If it were biological or neurological, changing the body would have solved the problem completely.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: By this I meant that it affected the way the brain works, which would make it both a biological and neurological weapon (Unless its memetic).

item.title: Re: Thanks, Attribution, and Others
item.content:encoded: the joke was that you forgot to include a comma between DrPrentice and Bluemorpho the first time

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love the idea of an SCP about babies refusing to be born, trying their hardest not to come into this forsaken reality. The direction this article takes with that idea, however, I don't find fulfilling enough for what it could be.

This is almost a +1 for me, but I have to stay my hand for now. There's something here that I have no doubts you can turn into greatness, and I look forward to see the refinement of it.

item.title: And this is now SCP-3434
item.content:encoded: Okay, it didn't win. But I actually like this number more; '3434' is more befitting of its content.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like the concept, and the writing is fine, but ultimately I wasn't interested enough by the execution. Certainly not bad, but not yet compelling enough for me to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is really good; I don't think there have been other SCP's that have made my jaw drop once I realized what was going on. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
CoBG neuro-bio weapon

What brought you to that conclusion? I am sincerely curious as to how you arrive at that.

I mean, the one who was killed by the Broken Church was the fifth person identified by the Foundation (and by no means, the first actual case). There can be others who were off-ed by other groups like the Daevites, Sarkics etc.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is this a CoBG neuro-bio weapon? Because it seems like that.

item.title: Re: Thanks, Attribution, and Others
item.content:encoded: It is? No no, there's another user called Prentice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
why is it Keter?

I'm operating under the 'Keter = it is not contained' definition here. IMO, it would only be Euclid if they find a way to redirect the appropriate souls to a source they can control for long periods of time.

item.title: Re: Thanks, Attribution, and Others
item.content:encoded: HOW DID YOU KNOW MY FIRST NAME WAS PRENTICE

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As others have said, this isn't particularly scary, but I like it all the same. I'll be upvoting after the contest ends.

I do have one other question, though: why is it Keter? It's uncontainable, certainly, but it doesn't appear to be particularly dangerous. Maybe the Foundation really wants to contain all instances for the purposes of maintaining secrecy, but I don't think that'd be Keter, just high-priority Euclid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
what makes the Foundation special enough that only their members reincarnate

Technically, that would be "what makes the Foundation special enough that only their members reincarnate with memories of previous lives intact."

'why do such different traumas produce the same stereotyped set of reactions'. It sort of ends up implicitly suggesting a unified theory of anomalies, which is a suggestion I never really like.

This certainly has no attempt towards the unified theory. The instances are effectively having PTSD, and the second behaviour relates to it. Perhaps the first behaviour (to not be in the light) is more unusually uniform. But I would argue that it was the darkness and loss of consciousness that relieved them of whatever suffering they went through prior to death, and that's really the one universal factor among almost all instances.

I don't buy that only Foundation people would come back, or would be traumatised so severely by those deaths that they're a special case.

Upon reflection, while the article does have the Foundation assume that the instances to be former Foundation personnel, the instances never explicitly declared that they are Foundation personnel (not to mention that not all instances are shown in the article). And besides, only one of the scenarios listed here can be tied directly to the Foundation (the nuke scenario). The rest can be decontextualised such that the person dying need not be Foundation personnel.

But whether the person is former Foundation member or not does not matter, for any instance might have important information regarding anomalies.

EDIT: In fact, the 1st and last scenario makes more sense if it is the Broken Church culling a heretical Ticker, instead of a Foundation member being ceremonially executed in a cruel and interesting way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Going to have to agree with TL333s; I was hyped going through the main body of the article, but my curiosity evaporated with the addenda.

I can't say I like the twist that these are reincarnated Foundation operatives. It invites a bunch of difficult to answer questions about where exactly the line between straightforward death and traumatic reincarnation lies, such as 'what makes the Foundation special enough that only their members reincarnate', 'why do so many different, seemingly unconnected anomalies all cause reincarnation', 'why do such different traumas produce the same stereotyped set of reactions'. It sort of ends up implicitly suggesting a unified theory of anomalies, which is a suggestion I never really like. It also kind of reminded me of Bell Weather, which I think gets away with the same thing because it's not constrained by the emphasis on explanations which the SCP format sets up, and because it straightforwardly implies that all Foundation personnel who die in the line of duty are cursed. This seems to be suggesting that dying for the Foundation just happens to be so awful that it traumatises the soul permanently, and given the examples (death by Brokenite torturers, death by absorption, blown up by experimental weapons, getting wished out of existence) I don't buy that only Foundation people would come back, or would be traumatised so severely by those deaths that they're a special case.

The fact that this is the last part of the article hampered the delivery. It starts of so well, with a strong idea and concept which both inspires curiosity and is deeply messed up. The question of 'what could scare an unborn child into resisting birth' is a fascinating one and works as a really strong hook into the second part of the article, and going into the addenda I expected this to be an easy upvote. Unfortunately, it felt like I was being given a bunch of vignettes which were neither strong enough to stand alone nor coherent enough to improve when taken together. Having all the deaths be due to other SCPs seemed like a little bit of a cop-out. I had expected going in that there would be new material, but the content that's given is a handful regurgitated older articles that seem to have been picked at random, with no real dramatic development within or across them save for -5 and -48 being the same person. The short interview snippets just become clipped retellings of other stories that I couldn't get much out of.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
But all of them being related to other skips was disappointing for reasons I can't quite explain. It felt like a rug-pull somehow.

I won't necessarily call it a rug-pull. Besides, two scenarios do not directly involve SCPs (and instead, other ways one can be KIA). Relating the deaths to other articles, I feel, expresses a message. These characters died for a greater good, to save the many by offering themselves. And in return, they suffer pain (forever).

And in the words of Monty Python's Life of Brian, while it is nice to be saved by a Messiah, being nailed onto a piece of wood and left to die is not good for the person being nailed.

Besides, it provides something to be said across the setting and this is the scale that is most deserving of this article's circumstances.

EDIT: Mind you, I can indeed conjure "original" scenarios (like the first and last one). However, I do not feel that the impact will be as resonating if all scenarios are things that a reader has no idea of. And frankly, the only reason why the first and last scenario is original is because I could not find a suitable death set in the early 1900s.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Downvote. The idea of fetuses that don't want to be born is fantastic, and the memory of dying was a good explanation, if expected. But all of them being related to other skips was disappointing for reasons I can't quite explain. It felt like a rug-pull somehow.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Huh.

The base anomaly here is rather simple, but how its explored through the logs is particularly interesting. Combined with some very unsettling body horror implied from the babies trying to avoid being born, and you have a pretty creepy entry.

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really love this. The interviews were the best part. (But I guess they often are).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh wow. It didn't really click until I actually saw the spoilers, but it's a very interesting concept. Similar to Scanny, it's not scary enough for me to upvote for the purposes of the contest, but it's a very cool story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't find this particularly scary (Then again, it's hard for me to get scared by written media, anyway), but it's well-written and well-executed enough that I don't care. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Remind me to upvote this after the contest. It's well-executed and fairly unsettling, but ultimately I just don't find it scary at all. So, not a 3000 contender, but still good.

item.title: Thanks, Attribution, and Others
item.content:encoded:
We die in the darkness, so that you may live in the light… Yeah, right!

This is my SCP-3000 proposal for this contest.

Firstly, allow me to express thanks to various reviewers like Gog33, Dr-Prentice, Bluemorpho, GibberingEloquence, LordStonefish, Targellon. Triple thanks to both psul and Petrograd, who took the trouble of looking at it thrice.

Image by Tormod Sandtrov of Flickr (https://www.flickr.com/photos/tormods/3841034148) under CC BY-SA 2.0; changes made include the addition of blackboxes

This article also involves the following:

  • Church of the Broken God hub by FortuneFavorsBold
  • SCP-428 (The Crowd) by DrScooter
  • SCP-343 ("God") by Unknown Author
  • I Thought You Died Alone by A Random Day

And as for spoilers,

+ show block
– hide block

The Foundation's position re: the instances is that they are Foundation members who died violently in-line of duty. Keyword is "Foundation's position", i.e. not the reality of it.

The instances definitely remember how they die, and that becomes their trauma even into their next lives.

Also, check the death date for -5 and birth date for -48. It's in dd/mm/yyyy formatting.
